slides.title: The pigment that turns salmon *pink*
slides.sub: Astaxanthin is the red carotenoid behind wild salmon's color, and one of nature's most studied antioxidants.

slides.title: Color with a purpose
slides.body: Salmon get their hue from algae rich in astaxanthin. The same pigment is a *fat-soluble antioxidant* the fish store in their tissue.

slides.title: It reaches the mitochondria
slides.body: Because it dissolves in fat, astaxanthin can settle into cell membranes and *reach the mitochondria*, where much oxidative stress begins.

slides.title: Quiet defense from UV
slides.body: Studies suggest astaxanthin *may help* the skin resist UV damage and hold moisture, supporting a more resilient look over time.

slides.title: Antioxidants and aging
slides.body: Oxidative stress is linked to how we age. Carotenoids like astaxanthin *can support* the body's own defenses against it.

slides.title: Eat the rainbow of the sea
slides.body: Wild salmon, trout, shrimp and krill carry astaxanthin naturally. A couple of *oily fish meals* a week is a simple start.

slides.title: Small habits, *tracked daily*.
slides.body: Vibe Health logs the food, sleep, movement and steps behind aging well, and turns them into one daily Health Score you can actually follow.
slides.cta_text: search ‘Vibe Health’ on the App Store

caption:
The pink in wild salmon is astaxanthin, a fat-soluble antioxidant that reaches your cells and skin. 🐟🌿

It can settle into your mitochondria, may help skin resist UV damage, and supports the body's defenses against oxidative aging. Eat oily fish a couple times a week. Save this. 🔖
